psychology.text: The 'door-in-the-face' technique triples compliance rates — first make a huge ask, then follow with the real (smaller) one.
Holding a warm cup of coffee makes people rate strangers as warmer personalities — physical and social warmth share the same neural circuitry.
Distributed practice over several days is 200% more effective than cramming the same total hours the night before an exam.
People make roughly 35,000 decisions per day, but only about 70 are conscious — the rest are automatic heuristics.
Forcing a smile — even by holding a pencil between your teeth — measurably reduces stress hormones and speeds cardiovascular recovery.
Generating information yourself (the 'generation effect') improves recall by 50% compared to simply reading the same content.
Writing about emotional experiences for 20 minutes improves immune function for up to 6 weeks afterward — Pennebaker's expressive writing effect.
The Pratfall Effect: a highly competent person becomes significantly more likeable after making a clumsy, embarrassing mistake.
Terror Management Theory finds that subtle reminders of death make people harsher judges of anyone who challenges their worldview.
Being ignored in an online chat with strangers for 3 minutes causes genuine psychological distress equal to being directly insulted.
Your name activates your auditory cortex even when spoken in a noisy crowd you're not consciously monitoring — the 'cocktail party effect.'
People in a good mood are systematically worse at detecting lies than people in a slightly negative mood — positivity reduces scrutiny.
People prefer a mirror-reversed photo of their own face — because that's the image they're used to seeing, not how others see them.
Reading literary fiction measurably increases theory of mind — the ability to infer others' mental states — more than reading nonfiction or popular fiction.
One sleepless night reduces emotional regulation by 60%, causing the amygdala to fire 3× more intensely to negative stimuli.
People underestimate how long tasks will take by an average of 40% — and this 'planning fallacy' persists even after being told about it.
Groups consistently underperform the sum of their members' individual abilities on complex problems — coordination overhead erases collective potential.
The 'spotlight effect' causes us to believe others notice our flaws and appearance approximately 3× more than they actually do.
Early childhood adversity raises the risk of adult depression by 46%, independent of genetic vulnerability.
Humans are the only species known to blush — Darwin called it 'the most peculiar and most human of all expressions.'
Intrinsic motivation collapses when external rewards are added — the 'overjustification effect' turns play into work.
The Halo Effect is so powerful that the same essay is rated higher quality when readers are told the author is physically attractive.
Adopting an expansive, open body posture for 2 minutes voluntarily reduces cortisol and increases willingness to take risks.
Flow states are associated with decreased prefrontal activity — peak performance involves less conscious control, not more.
The average person has 4–5 close friends; having fewer carries health risks equivalent to smoking 15 cigarettes a day.
Emotional support from strangers during a crisis is often rated as more helpful than support from friends, who tend to minimize the threat.
Autobiographical memory is reconstructive, not reproductive — every recall subtly alters the original memory through reconsolidation.
Psychology findings disproportionately come from WEIRD populations (Western, Educated, Industrialized, Rich, Democratic) who represent 12% of the world.
Silence longer than 4 seconds in conversation triggers anxiety in both speakers — an evolutionary signal that something is socially wrong.
Six basic facial expressions — happiness, sadness, fear, disgust, anger, surprise — are recognized universally, even in isolated cultures with no Western media.
When you give others advice about a problem you also face, you become more motivated to act on that advice yourself — the 'self-persuasion' effect.
Social rejection activates the same brain region as physical pain (dACC) — and Tylenol reduces both.
Eye contact held for more than 3.2 seconds is perceived as threatening regardless of the social context.
People who write a to-do list before bed fall asleep an average of 9 minutes faster than those who rehearse completed tasks.
Affective forecasting is systematically wrong — people overestimate how long negative events will hurt them by a factor of 3 or more ('impact bias').
Red clothing increases perceived attractiveness and dominance across multiple unrelated cultures — the effect holds in photos with color swapped.
During reading, the mind wanders every 13–17 seconds on average — even during solitary, self-paced tasks.
The 'Benjamin Franklin Effect': asking someone a small favor makes them like you more, not less — they rationalize their helpfulness as genuine affection.
Children at 14 months spontaneously share food with strangers and help adults reach objects — prosocial behavior precedes language.
Handedness is established in the womb by 8 weeks — before limbs connect to the brain — suggesting genetic expression precedes neural wiring.
Nostalgia functions as a psychological buffer against loneliness, increasing feelings of social connectedness and self-continuity.
Romantic partners unconsciously synchronize walking pace, breathing rate, and heartbeat within minutes of close proximity.
Mental imagery and actual perception share 90% of their neural substrate — the brain barely distinguishes imagined from seen.
People who forgive others' mistakes show lower cortisol responses to social stress and faster cardiovascular recovery.
The 'Pygmalion Effect': when teachers were falsely told students were gifted, those students scored measurably higher on IQ tests within one year.
Humor in a negotiation reduces impasse rates by 28% without reducing outcome quality — laughter signals trust, not distraction.
'Emotional granularity' — distinguishing subtle emotions — is a learnable skill; high scorers show smaller amygdala responses to stress.
Shame and guilt activate different brain regions: guilt is adaptive (motivates repair); shame is self-focused and correlates with psychopathology.
The 'peak-end rule': we judge experiences by their emotional peak and how they ended, not by their average — a long mediocre experience feels shorter than a brief bad one if it ended well.
Daydreaming about the future (prospection) recruits the identical neural network as remembering the past (retrospection) — the default mode network.
Social loafing — reduced effort in groups — vanishes when individual contributions are made identifiable, even anonymously to the person alone.
LSD and psilocybin reduce activity in the default mode network — 'ego dissolution' correlates with decreased DMN connectivity, not increased activity.
The recency effect produces better recall of recently learned items in immediate tests; but in delayed tests, primacy items (first learned) are recalled better.
People who believe they have a growth mindset but don't actually practice deliberate effort show no academic advantage over those who don't.
The 'false uniqueness effect': people believe their abilities and virtues are rarer than they actually are — the inverse of the false consensus effect.
Voluntary recall of a positive memory while deliberately adopting an upright posture measurably reduces cortisol within 2 minutes.
Teleological thinking — attributing purpose to random events — is a universal cognitive bias that emerges in children before age 3.
Crying has different neurochemistry depending on its trigger; tears of sadness, joy, and empathy have distinct protein compositions.
People detect submissiveness vs. dominance from footsteps alone in under 100ms — before any visual information is available.
'Emotional contagion' is involuntary — humans unconsciously mimic others' facial expressions in 200ms and experience the same emotion as a result.
The mind-body connection runs both ways: maintaining good posture during a stressful task keeps testosterone higher and cortisol lower than slumping.
People with higher 'need for cognition' are more resistant to misinformation but also more prone to over-analyzing simple decisions to their detriment.

cognitiveBiases.text: The Dunning-Kruger effect is widely misquoted — it doesn't say incompetent people feel like experts; it says low performers are less accurate at self-assessment than high performers.
Anchoring bias is so powerful that judges assign shorter sentences when prosecutors request low numbers, even when the request is made arbitrarily.
The 'IKEA effect': people value objects they partially assembled themselves 63% higher than pre-assembled identical items.
Loss aversion makes losses feel 2–2.5× more painful than equivalent gains feel pleasurable — asymmetry that drives most financial irrationality.
The framing effect: patients choose surgery at 90% when framed as '90% survival,' but reject it when identically framed as '10% mortality.'
The availability heuristic causes people to overestimate dramatic causes of death (plane crashes, sharks) and underestimate mundane ones (falls, heart disease).
Confirmation bias is stronger for political beliefs than factual ones — people accept false political facts without checking, but challenge neutral ones.
Hindsight bias reconstructs memory within seconds of an unpredictable event — people genuinely believe they 'knew it all along.'
The 'hot hand fallacy' is partially real in basketball — player confidence after a streak changes shot selection and forces tighter defense.
Optimism bias affects 80% of people — we believe we're less likely than others to experience divorce, cancer, or job loss, regardless of base rates.
The planning fallacy persists even when you've personally overrun budgets before and have been explicitly told about the bias.
The endowment effect makes us demand 2–3× more to give up something than we'd pay to acquire it — mere ownership inflates perceived value.
The decoy effect: adding an obviously inferior option to a menu increases sales of the premium option by making it appear more reasonable by comparison.
Status quo bias makes inaction feel 'safe' even when the status quo has a clear, measurable cost — it's the default that needs no justification.
The clustering illusion causes humans to see meaningful patterns in random sequences almost every time — we have no intuition for true randomness.
The sunk cost fallacy affects rats — they press an unproductive lever harder if they've previously invested effort pressing it.
The telescoping effect distorts time: recent events feel further away than they are; distant events feel more recent — retrospective time perception warps.
The false consensus effect: people systematically assume their opinions, behaviors, and preferences are more common than they actually are.
Inattentional blindness is so powerful that 50% of observers fail to see a person in a gorilla suit walking through a basketball game they're tracking.
Outcome bias causes doctors, coaches, and investors to be judged on results even when their decision under uncertainty was perfectly rational.
Temporal discounting is hyperbolic, not exponential — we're disproportionately impatient about near-term delays, but almost indifferent between 10 and 11 years from now.
The 'curse of knowledge' makes experts structurally unable to predict how long a novice will take to learn something — they cannot unlearn.
Belief perseverance: once formed, beliefs resist change even after the original supporting evidence is retracted or fully disproved.
Zero-risk bias causes people to prefer eliminating a small risk entirely over reducing a much larger risk by a greater amount — the basis of most safety theater.
Proportionality bias makes people assume large, dramatic events must have large causes — one reason conspiracy theories flourish around major events.
The contrast effect means the same object seems better or worse depending purely on what was perceived immediately before it.
In-group favoritism persists even when group membership is arbitrary — being randomly assigned to 'Group A' generates measurable bias against 'Group B.'
Overconfidence bias peaks in domains where people have moderate knowledge — genuine experts are the most accurately calibrated group.
Base rate neglect: people ignore statistical base rates in favor of vivid case descriptions, even when given the exact numbers up front.
The fundamental attribution error is culturally specific — East Asian cultures show far weaker version, attributing behavior more to situational context.
Restraint bias: people consistently overestimate their ability to resist temptation in future situations, which is why dieters bring chips home.
The 'Google effect': knowing you can look something up makes you less likely to encode it into long-term memory during the initial encounter.
Naive realism holds that we perceive the world objectively and that anyone who disagrees is biased — a universal belief that is almost never examined.
Action bias in soccer: goalkeepers dive left or right on 94% of penalties even though staying center is statistically the most effective response.
Fluency effect: information presented in a harder-to-read font is rated as less true and less likely to occur than the identical text in an easy font.
Stereotyping activates in under 100ms — before conscious categorization is possible, automatic associations already bias perception.
Projection bias: we project our current emotional state onto future preferences, which is why grocery shopping while hungry reliably leads to overspending.
Moral luck: identical decisions are judged more harshly when they happen to lead to a bad outcome — the outcome changes the perceived ethics of the choice.
The empathy gap: people in a calm state consistently underestimate how differently they will behave when emotionally aroused — the 'hot-cold' problem.
The rhyme-as-reason effect: aphorisms that rhyme are rated as more accurate and insightful than equivalent non-rhyming versions of the same idea.
Worse-than-average effect co-exists with better-than-average: for difficult tasks, most people rate themselves below average — accuracy inverts by difficulty.
Social proof drives 85% of participants to give at least one clearly wrong answer to match a group, even when the correct answer is unambiguous (Asch, 1955).
The peak-end rule: the remembered quality of an experience depends on its emotional peak and its ending, not on the average — duration is nearly irrelevant.
Attentional bias toward threat means people with anxiety literally scan environments differently — their eyes find threat cues before neutral information.
Information bias: people prefer more information even when additional data demonstrably cannot improve a decision.
Neglect of probability: people treat very small probabilities as effectively zero or near-certain, with no reliable intuition for anything in between.
Automation bias: people trust automated system outputs more than human judgment, even when the machine is visibly and clearly wrong.
The illusion of explanatory depth: people vastly overestimate how well they can explain systems they use daily — zippers, flush toilets, policy proposals.
Choice overload: 24 jam options produced 60% more stops but 90% fewer purchases than a display of 6 jams — more options paralyzed buyers.
Reactive devaluation: proposals lose appeal simply because they come from the opposing side — the same peace deal is rated worse if the enemy offers it.
The denomination effect: people spend more money when carrying small bills than large bills of the same total — breaking a big bill feels like a larger loss.
Agent detection bias: humans evolved to see faces and intentions in ambiguous patterns — false positives were always cheaper than missing a real predator.
Scope insensitivity: people donate nearly the same amount to save 2,000 birds as 200,000 birds — emotional response does not scale with magnitude.
Illusory correlation: people see relationships between variables that don't exist, especially when both are salient, unusual, or emotionally charged.
The certainty effect: people weight certain outcomes more heavily than probabilities with identical expected value — certainty is psychologically special.
Myside bias: people reason more carefully about claims that challenge their position — they spot opposing weaknesses faster than their own.
Leveling and sharpening: in rumor transmission, stories lose peripheral detail (leveling) while central elements become exaggerated (sharpening) within 3 retellings.
The 'just-world hypothesis' causes people to blame victims for their misfortune — a coping mechanism for the anxiety of random harm.
Attribute substitution: when a hard question is asked, the brain silently substitutes an easier question and answers that instead, without noticing.
The 'what the hell' effect: once dieters exceed their daily limit by any amount, they eat dramatically more — they abandon the goal rather than accept a minor deviation.
Moral licensing: doing something virtuous (recycling, donating) measurably increases willingness to behave selfishly or unethically shortly afterward.
The fluency heuristic extends to faces — people who are easy to visually process are rated as more trustworthy, even in 100ms exposures.

neuroscience.text: The brain consumes 20% of the body's energy despite being only 2% of its mass — it's the most metabolically expensive organ by a large margin.
A single neuron can form up to 10,000 synaptic connections with other neurons — the brain contains an estimated 100–500 trillion synapses.
The human brain generates 12–25 watts of electrical power — enough to dim an LED bulb.
Myelin increases nerve signal speed from ~1 m/s to over 100 m/s — a 100× amplification from a fatty sheath.
The cerebellum contains more neurons than the entire rest of the brain combined — about 69 billion of the brain's 86 billion total.
The adult hippocampus generates approximately 700 new neurons per day — neurogenesis continues throughout life, shaped by exercise and stress.
The visual cortex actively predicts what it expects to see and only updates when predictions fail — perception is top-down inference, not bottom-up recording.
Pain is not a signal from the body — it is a construction of the brain using body signals as one input among many.
Octopus arms contain independent neural ganglia; each arm can taste, touch, and respond to stimuli without instruction from the central brain.
Sleep spindles during NREM sleep transfer memories from the hippocampus to the neocortex for long-term storage — the mechanism of overnight consolidation.
Microglia (the brain's immune cells) prune synapses during adolescence — over-pruning in specific circuits is a leading hypothesis for schizophrenia onset.
Phantom limb pain occurs because the cortical map reorganizes after amputation, reassigning the missing limb's territory to neighboring body parts.
The default mode network (DMN) is more metabolically active during rest than during focused tasks — doing nothing is neurologically expensive.
The frontal lobes finish myelinating at age 25 — structural incompleteness in adolescence is the biological basis for impulsive risk-taking.
Mirror neurons fire both during action and during observation of that action — the same cell that fires when you grab an object fires when you watch someone else grab it.
GABA is the brain's primary inhibitory neurotransmitter — at any moment, most of the brain is actively inhibiting most of the rest of itself.
If unfolded, the cerebral cortex would cover approximately 2,500 cm² — the size of an office desktop — despite being tightly folded inside the skull.
The olfactory bulb connects directly to the amygdala and hippocampus without a thalamic relay — why smells trigger stronger emotional memories than any other sense.
The blood-brain barrier is absent in the hypothalamus, pituitary, and area postrema — these windows allow hormone and toxin sampling from the bloodstream.
Dopamine is released in anticipation of a reward, not primarily upon receiving it — the wanting system drives behavior more than the satisfaction system.
The amygdala initiates fight-or-flight via a subcortical 'low road' in as little as 12ms — before visual cortex even finishes processing the image.
Hebbian plasticity ('neurons that fire together, wire together') underlies all associative learning and forms the conceptual basis of backpropagation in neural networks.
The hippocampus is a memory indexer, not a storage site — it binds distributed cortical representations into coherent episodes, then gradually becomes unnecessary.
Chronic stress suppresses hippocampal neurogenesis — sustained cortisol literally shrinks hippocampal volume by reducing new cell formation.
Cortical folding patterns are as unique as fingerprints — no two human brains have identical sulcal geometry.
REM sleep paralyzes the body (atonia) to prevent acting out dreams — people with REM behavior disorder physically thrash, kick, and vocalise during REM.
The nucleus accumbens is activated by food, sex, music, and financial gain — using the identical dopaminergic circuitry for every category of reward.
Synesthesia (hearing colors, seeing sounds) is not imagination — it produces genuine measurable cross-cortical activation confirmed by fMRI and MEG.
The opioid system mediates the 'liking' of rewards; dopamine mediates the 'wanting' — you can want something intensely while not liking it at all.
Gamma oscillations at 40 Hz are associated with conscious experience — their disruption is a mechanistic link in disorders of consciousness.
The orbitofrontal cortex encodes 'reward prediction error' — it signals the gap between expected and actual outcome, not the outcome itself.
Testosterone increases economic risk-taking but reduces decision quality — more boldness does not produce better outcomes in uncertain environments.
The lateral habenula encodes disappointment — it actively suppresses dopamine firing when rewards are worse than expected, driving avoidance behavior.
The human brain is approximately 60% fat by dry weight — it is the fattiest organ in the body.
The gut microbiome produces 95% of the body's serotonin — most serotonin lives in the gut, not the brain, and regulates motility more than mood.
Oxytocin promotes trust and bonding toward in-group members while simultaneously increasing hostility and suspicion toward out-group members.
Long-term potentiation (LTP), first demonstrated by Bliss and Lømo in 1973, remains the leading cellular model of how memory is physically encoded.
The insular cortex processes signals from inside the body (interoception) and is the neural substrate of 'gut feelings' and physiological self-awareness.
Traumatic memories are encoded with stronger amygdala activation but weaker hippocampal context binding — vivid but decontextualized fragments.
Neural Darwinism (Edelman, 1987) proposes that synaptic connections compete for survival through use-dependent selection — evolution occurring in real time within a single brain.
Serotonin's role in depression is overstated — SSRIs' mechanism remains contested; depleting serotonin in healthy people does not reliably produce depression.
Neuroinflammation is increasingly implicated in depression — patients with treatment-resistant depression show elevated microglial activation on PET scans.
The cingulate cortex contains von Economo spindle neurons found only in humans and great apes — uniquely wired for social and moral decision-making.
Adrenaline released after learning strengthens memory consolidation — emotional arousal after an event, not during it, is what makes memory vivid.
Brain lateralization is not binary — most cognitive functions are bilaterally represented, with left or right dominance only for specific sub-processes.
The hypothalamus is the size of a pea yet simultaneously controls hunger, thirst, sleep, body temperature, sexual drive, and circadian rhythm.
Ketamine produces antidepressant effects within hours by blocking NMDA glutamate receptors — a fundamentally different mechanism from all prior antidepressants.
Spreading cortical depression — a wave of electrical silence sweeping across cortex — is now the established mechanism of migraine aura.
Adult hippocampal neurogenesis may not occur in humans at all — a 2018 UCSF study found no new neurons in adult hippocampus, contradicting decades of rodent data.
The brain's glymphatic waste clearance system is 10× more active during sleep — poor sleep literally allows metabolic waste to accumulate in brain tissue.
GABAergic interneurons synchronize local neural populations — disrupting this synchrony is a proposed shared mechanism in both autism and schizophrenia.
The prefrontal cortex has the highest density of dopamine receptors of any brain region, making it the most vulnerable to stress-induced dopamine depletion.
Action potentials range from 0.5 m/s in unmyelinated fibers to 120 m/s in large myelinated motor neurons — a 200× speed variation within one brain.
The fusiform face area recognizes a familiar face in a crowd in under 100ms — faster than any other object category.
Amygdala gray matter volume correlates positively with the size and social complexity of one's real-world social network.
The human visual system has more cortical processing dedicated to the lower visual field than the upper — evolved to track ground-based threats.
White matter (myelinated axons) constitutes about 50% of total brain volume but is largely ignored in most cognitive neuroscience imaging studies.
The 'binding problem' — how the brain integrates separate features (color, shape, motion) into a unified perceptual object — remains fundamentally unsolved.
Infant brains show cross-modal responses to all sensory categories simultaneously; specialization is carved out through experience, not pre-set at birth.
The brainstem controls breathing, heart rate, and swallowing entirely automatically — these life-sustaining functions never require conscious initiation.
Stroke recovery can occur years post-injury via neuroplasticity — unused cortical areas can be recruited to take over lost functions through intensive rehab.
Psilocybin and LSD reduce default mode network activity — the sense of 'ego dissolution' correlates with DMN suppression, not with psychedelic activation.

experiments.text: Milgram (1963): 65% of participants delivered what they believed were lethal 450-volt shocks when an authority figure simply said 'please continue.'
Stanford Prison Experiment (Zimbardo, 1971): volunteer 'guards' became so abusive the study was halted after 6 days of a planned 2-week run.
Asch Conformity (1951–56): 75% of participants gave at least one obviously wrong answer to match the group — the correct line was clearly longer.
Bobo Doll (Bandura, 1961): children who watched an adult punch an inflatable clown precisely replicated the aggression — including inventing novel aggressive acts.
Little Albert (Watson, 1920): a 9-month-old was conditioned to fear a white rat in one session, then generalized the fear to fur coats and Santa Claus masks.
Hawthorne Effect (1920s): any change in factory conditions increased output — workers improved because they were being observed, not because of the specific change.
Marshmallow Test (Mischel, 1972): 4-year-olds who waited scored higher on SATs 14 years later — but replications show SES is the confounding predictor, not willpower.
Harlow's Monkeys (1950s): infant rhesus monkeys chose a soft cloth surrogate over a wire one that provided milk — contact comfort beats nutrition.
Bystander Effect (Darley & Latané, 1968): in a room filling with smoke, 75% of alone subjects reported it in 2 minutes; only 10% did when with two passive strangers.
Cognitive Dissonance (Festinger, 1959): participants paid $1 to lie rated a boring task as more enjoyable than those paid $20 — small pay forces attitude change.
Asian Disease Problem (Kahneman & Tversky, 1981): identical policy options produced opposite choices based only on 'lives saved' vs. 'lives lost' framing.
Learned Helplessness (Seligman, 1967): dogs given inescapable shocks stopped trying to escape even after escape became trivially possible.
On Being Sane in Insane Places (Rosenhan, 1973): 8 pseudopatients gained psychiatric admission by faking one hallucination, then were held 7–52 days while behaving normally.
Blue Eyes/Brown Eyes (Elliott, 1968): 3rd-graders given arbitrary privilege became dismissive of 'inferior' peers and scored better academically — within one day.
Good Samaritan Study (1973): seminary students rushing to give a lecture on the Good Samaritan parable stepped over a visibly distressed stranger 90% of the time.
Clark & Clark Doll Studies (1940s): Black children as young as 3 preferred white dolls and called them 'nice' — cited as evidence in Brown v. Board of Education.
Linda Problem (Tversky & Kahneman, 1983): 85% said Linda was more likely a 'feminist bank teller' than a 'bank teller' — violating basic probability.
Misinformation Effect (Loftus, 1974): asking 'how fast were the cars when they smashed?' caused subjects to hallucinate broken glass that wasn't in the video.
Operant Conditioning (Skinner): variable-ratio reinforcement — like slot machines — produces the highest and most extinction-resistant response rate of all schedules.
When Prophecy Fails (Festinger, 1956): when a doomsday cult's predicted apocalypse didn't arrive, believers became more fervent missionaries — dissonance drove evangelism.
Robbers Cave (Sherif, 1954): two groups of 11-year-old boys developed intense hostility in 3 days; it was resolved only by superordinate goals requiring cooperation.
Overjustification (Deci, 1971): children paid to draw with markers they loved stopped drawing voluntarily the moment payment was withdrawn.
Stroop Effect (1935): naming the ink color of the word 'BLUE' written in red takes measurably longer than naming a neutral word's color — automaticity interferes.
Autokinetic Effect (Sherif, 1936): a stationary light in the dark appears to move — group norms for its 'movement' persisted even when participants were tested alone.
Magical Number 7 (Miller, 1956): working memory holds 7±2 chunks — this paper still underlies the length of phone numbers in most countries.
Taxi Cab Problem (Tversky & Kahneman, 1982): subjects said a blue cab was probably green despite 80% witness accuracy, because they ignored the 85% base rate of green cabs.
Classical Conditioning (Pavlov, 1890s): salivation at the sound of a bell was accidentally discovered when Pavlov noticed dogs drooling at lab assistants before food appeared.
Libet's Free Will (1983): the brain's readiness potential began 550ms before subjects reported a conscious intention to move — raising questions about the timing of volition.
Milgram Variation: obedience dropped from 65% to 10% when authority commands came via telephone rather than from someone physically present in the room.
Ekman Cross-Cultural (1960s–70s): Papua New Guineans with no Western media exposure identified the same six facial expressions as American undergraduates.
Thorndike's Puzzle Boxes (1898): cats escaped by trial-and-error, not insight — establishing the Law of Effect decades before Skinner's formalization.
Jigsaw Classroom (Aronson, 1971): cooperative learning reduced racial prejudice, improved grades, and increased school liking among minority students within 6 weeks.
Expressive Writing (Pennebaker, 1986): writing about trauma for 15 minutes over 4 days reduced physician visits in the following 6 months — immune function improved.
Sauce Béarnaise Study (Garcia & Koelling, 1966): rats learned to avoid a flavor paired with nausea after a single trial separated by 6 hours — violating standard conditioning rules.
Halo Effect (Thorndike, 1920): military officers rated soldiers as uniformly good or bad across all traits — a single impression colored every subsequent judgment.
Weapons Effect (Berkowitz & LePage, 1967): the mere presence of a gun in the room increased aggressive behavior in mildly provoked subjects.
Candle Problem (Duncker, 1945): subjects took 3.5 minutes longer to solve a task when materials were labeled vs. presented unlabeled — functional fixedness in action.
Cheater Detection (Cosmides & Tooby, 1992): people solved social-violation logic puzzles 4× faster than identical abstract ones — a domain-specific reasoning module.
Strange Situation (Ainsworth, 1969): 4 infant attachment styles — secure, anxious-ambivalent, avoidant, disorganized — identified and still used as the standard framework.
Bahrick Retention Studies: vocabulary from a foreign language declines steeply for 3–6 years then levels off for 25+ years — a 'permastore' of core knowledge.
Noise & Control (Glass & Singer, 1972): uncontrollable noise impaired performance — but merely knowing controllable noise could occur was enough to produce the same impairment.
Piaget Conservation (1950s): children under 7 believe pouring water into a taller, thinner glass creates more water — they lack the concept that transformations preserve quantity.
Encoding Specificity (Tulving, 1970s): memory retrieval was 40% better when the environment at recall matched the environment at encoding — even mood counts.
Lost Letter Experiment (Milgram, 1977): more 'found' letters were mailed when addressed to respectable organizations than to controversial ones — prosocial behavior is selective.
Smoky Room (1968): 75% of alone subjects reported smoke in 2 minutes; with two passive confederates present, only 10% reported it in 6 minutes — social proof overrides danger.
McGurk Effect (1976): watching a mouth say 'ga' while hearing 'ba' produces the perception of 'da' — vision overwrites audition, not the other way around.
Demand Characteristics (Orne, 1962): subjects endure extreme boredom or pain to 'help' the researcher — compliance bias distorts nearly every lab finding.
Lewin Leadership (1939): autocratic-led groups were more productive but more hostile; democratic groups were slightly less productive but far more creative and satisfied.
Festinger Boring Task (1959): attitude change from induced compliance requires the behavior to be freely chosen — forced compliance does not create genuine belief change.
Invisible Gorilla (Simons & Chabris, 1999): told to count basketball passes, 50% of observers failed to notice a person in a gorilla suit walking through the game.
Spacing Effect (Ebbinghaus, 1885): spaced repetition produces 200% better retention than equivalent massed practice — the oldest finding in memory research still massively underused.
Terror Management (Greenberg et al., 1986): subtle death reminders caused judges to assign significantly higher bail amounts to a prostitute to defend against mortality awareness.
